text.title: Deprecation, Sunset, and Lifecycle
text.summary: Deprecation/Sunset response headers, per-version usage metrics, oasdiff CI gate, and sunset enforcement via 410 Gone.

rule.statement: Emit Deprecation, Sunset, and Link: rel=successor-version headers from every deprecated route from the day deprecation is announced.
rule.rationale: Headers are machine-readable. SDK maintainers and observability tools can detect them automatically. Blog posts alone are not sufficient for long-tail mobile clients.

example.code: HTTP/1.1 200 OK Deprecation: Sun, 01 Jan 2026 00:00:00 GMT Sunset: Sun, 01 Jul 2026 00:00:00 GMT Link: </api/v2/users>; rel="successor-version"

rule.statement: Track per-version traffic via a Prometheus label (http_requests_total{version="v1"}). Do not sunset a version until traffic is zero or negligible for 30 days.
rule.rationale: You cannot sunset what you cannot measure. Removing a version with active clients causes immediate incidents, regardless of announced sunset dates.

rule.statement: On the sunset date, routes return 410 Gone with a body pointing to v2. Delete the handler code only in a separate PR after 30 days of zero traffic.
rule.rationale: Two-PR sunset: (1) 410 Gone, (2) code deletion. This gives agents and clients final warning before code disappears and prevents accidental restoration.

antipattern.description: Agent deduplicates v1/v2 handler code to be "DRY".
antipattern.reason: Shared code silently introduces v1 breaking changes when v2 evolves. v1 routes must be frozen in a separate, read-only module.
Requires a per-customer version pin + request/response transformation pipeline. Overhead is not paid back at small scale. URL path versioning is the right default.
